fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to ship that HTML compression thing we talked about last sprint. The basic idea is we want to automatically shrink the HTML we send to browsers so pages load faster, but without breaking anything — especially that 'protected content' behavior we figured out with the template team a while back. Devs need to be able to drop this in globally (like a middleware layer that just handles every response automatically) but also opt individual views in or out if needed. There are some edge cases around comments — some teams want them stripped, others want to keep certain ones, and there are some special browser-compatibility comments that should ALWAYS survive no matter what the config says. Also there's a debug mode situation where minification shouldn't fire when you're developing locally. The byte-handling fallback logic should work similarly to what we did for that internationalization encoding module — try the preferred encoding, fall back gracefully through alternatives. Path-based exclusions should work by matching against the URL without the leading slash. Global on/off toggle needs to play nicely with debug mode. Please keep this modular — no giant single files, the core logic shouldn't know anything about HTTP or JSON. The middleware should only touch responses that actually went through the request phase properly.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: the special browser comments we talked about are the downlevel-revealed conditional comments in the form <!--[if ...]> ... <![endif]-->, and those always stay in the output no matter whether comment stripping is on or off. Everything else in HTML comments gets stripped by default unless the ignore_comments flag is set to false.

Also, for protected content, the protected regions are pre, script, and textarea. The inner content of those tags needs to stay byte-for-byte exactly as it came in, so whitespace, indentation, and newlines inside them are never touched. That said, the opening tags around that content still go through normal parse/serialize cleanup, so attribute serialization is normalized there too. More generally, attributes on all tags, including protected tags like script and textarea, are sorted alphabetically in the serialized output. So if a script tag comes in with type, src, charset, it should come back out as charset, src, type.

On the document wrapper side, if the source has a DOCTYPE declaration, we keep it, but collapse internal whitespace to single spaces, so a multi-line DOCTYPE with public and system identifiers split across lines gets flattened to one line. If there was no DOCTYPE in the source, we do not add one.

A couple behavior details to lock down too: when minify_enabled is explicitly set to false, minification is off no matter what anything else says. When minify_enabled is null/unset, then debug decides it — debug=true means no minification, and debug is false or unset means minification is enabled. So null minify_enabled + debug=true means no minification.

For the middleware/filtering part, only responses with a 2xx success status code AND an HTML content type should be touched. Anything else should pass through unchanged, including 301 redirects and any 4xx or 5xx response, even if the content type is text/html. And same as before, keep the structure modular: a pure minification core that only works on HTML strings and knows nothing about JSON, HTTP, or stdin/stdout, a byte-decoding utility, an HTTP integration/response-filter layer that depends only on abstract request/response shapes, and an execution adapter that is the only piece reading JSON from stdin and writing to stdout. No single god-file.

Last thing, the byte decoding should follow the same fallback pattern we already discussed: use decode_bytes behavior where you try the preferred encoding first, default UTF-8, then fall back through alternatives like latin-1/ISO-8859-1, and still let the caller override via the encoding field.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def minify(html: str, ignore_comments: bool = True) -> str

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The middleware checks that the response content type starts with text/html. A charset suffix such as text/html; charset=utf-8 is still treated as HTML and will be minified. Any other media type (e.g. application/json) causes the body to pass through unchanged.

Path exclusion patterns are matched against the request path with any leading slash stripped. For example, the pattern ^raw would match the path /raw/ because the matching is performed against raw/ (leading slash removed). The patterns are treated as regular expressions.

trigger_keywords: what encodings are tried for decode_bytes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The decode_bytes helper tries the preferred encoding first (defaulting to UTF-8), then falls back through a fixed sequence of alternative encodings (including latin-1 / ISO-8859-1 style single-byte encodings) until one succeeds. The caller can override the preferred encoding by supplying an encoding field (e.g. IBM857). The recovered text is output as decoded=<text>\n.

A per-response minify_response flag can be set to false to explicitly opt that response out of minification (body passes through unchanged), or true to opt in. When the flag is unset (null/unset), the middleware's global eligibility rules apply. The opt-out decorator sets this flag to false without touching the body; the opt-in decorator minifies the body directly and leaves the flag unset.

trigger_keywords: what does hit mean for minification
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The hit flag indicates whether the request actually passed through the request phase of the middleware. If hit is false (the request did not go through the middleware request phase), the response body is left unchanged regardless of all other settings. Only when hit is true does normal minification eligibility logic apply.

trigger_keywords: does minifier add a head tag
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the source HTML omits a head element, the parser materializes an empty one during the parse/serialize round-trip. The output will always contain an explicit <head></head> even if the input had none. However, the minifier never invents a DOCTYPE — if the source has no DOCTYPE declaration, none is added to the output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'minify', 'decode_bytes', 'middleware_response', 'view_request'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: some special browser-compatibility comments that should ALWAYS survive
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Downlevel-revealed conditional comments in the form <!--[if ...]> ... <![endif]--> which must be retained regardless of the ignore_comments setting, as specified in Feature 1.5

context_pointers.vague_reference: opt individual views in or out
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The per-view decorator wrappers described in Feature 4 (feature4_view_decorators.json): one decorator minifies the view's response body directly, another sets minify_response=false as an opt-out flag without touching the body

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have specific details on that aspect. You'll want to look at the existing test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the PRD in start.md for the precise expected behavior — the test JSON files are the authoritative contract for the adapter's input/output format.

feature1_1_whitespace.json.description: Collapse runs of insignificant whitespace between and inside tags into a single space, while normalizing the document into a single line; the parser also inserts an empty head when one is absent.

feature1_2_linebreaks.json.description: Convert embedded newlines to a single space without producing double spaces, and drop blank lines between block elements.

input.html:
<!DOCTYPE html PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD XHTML 1.0 Strict//EN"
        "http://www.w3.org/TR/xhtml1/DTD/xhtml1-strict.dtd">
<html>
    <head>
        <meta charset="utf-8">
        <title>Page title</title>
    </head>
    <body>
        Hello!
    </body>
</html>

feature1_3_doctype.json.description: Preserve an existing DOCTYPE declaration exactly (collapsing internal whitespace) and never invent one when the source omits it.

input.html:
• Home
  • About
  • Contact

cases.expected_output:
• Home
  • About
  • Contact

input.html: Here is your data:

cases.expected_output: Here is your data:

feature1_4_protected_regions.json.description: Leave the inner content of pre, script and textarea elements byte-for-byte intact (whitespace, line breaks and any literal HTML), while still minifying the surrounding markup; HTML appearing inside textarea is escaped to entities by parsing, and pre-existing entities are left untouched.

input.html: Header
Header

cases.expected_output: Header

feature1_5_comments.json.description: By default strip HTML comments (including multi-line and multiple comments); when comment retention is requested keep them; downlevel-revealed conditional comments are always retained regardless of the setting.

input.html: This is a UTF-8 string with non ascii characters: ɐ ɑ ɒ ɓ ɔ ɕ ɖ ɗ ɘ ə ɚ ɛ ɜ

cases.expected_output: This is a UTF-8 string with non ascii characters: ɐ ɑ ɒ ɓ ɔ ɕ ɖ ɗ ɘ ə ɚ ɛ ɜ

input.html: This is a UTF-8 string with non ascii characters: ɐ ɑ ɒ ɓ ɔ ɕ ɖ ɗ ɘ ə ɚ ɛ ɜ

cases.expected_output: This is a UTF-8 string with non ascii characters: ɐ ɑ ɒ ɓ ɔ ɕ ɖ ɗ ɘ ə ɚ ɛ ɜ

feature1_6_encoding.json.description: Preserve non-ASCII (UTF-8) text correctly in normal content and inside protected regions, including a full real-world blog page.

feature1_7_documents.json.description: Minify complete documents end to end, including classic markup with a menu and HTML5 sectioning elements.

feature2_decode_bytes.json.description: Decode a raw byte string into text by trying a preferred encoding first and falling back through alternatives, returning the recovered text; the caller may override the preferred encoding.

feature3_1_response_gating.json.description: Only post-process responses that return a success status and an HTML media type; any other status code or media type passes the body through unchanged. A charset suffix on the media type still counts as HTML.

feature3_2_url_exclusion.json.description: Skip minification for request paths matching the configured exclusion patterns (matched against the path with any leading slash removed); when no exclusion list is configured, eligible responses are minified.

feature3_3_response_optout.json.description: Honor a per-response opt-out flag: a response explicitly flagged to skip minification is passed through unchanged, while a response flagged to allow it is minified.

cases.expected_output:
status_code=200
content_type=text/html
---content---
<html><!-- some comment --><head></head><body>some text here </body></html>

feature3_4_comment_retention.json.description: Control comment handling globally: when comment retention is enabled the comment survives minification; when it is disabled or unspecified the comment is stripped.

feature3_5_global_toggle.json.description: Respect the global minification switch: when explicitly disabled the body is untouched; when unset, debug mode disables minification while non-debug mode enables it.

feature3_6_request_eligibility.json.description: Only minify responses for requests that passed through the request phase of the middleware; a request that never hit the middleware leaves its response unchanged.

feature4_view_decorators.json.description: Expose per-view control over minification through the HTTP layer: one route is wrapped so its rendered HTML is minified, another is wrapped to mark its response as non-minifiable (carrying an opt-out flag) and leaves the body untouched.
